utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality reports that passed all verifier checks and satisfied all 7 evaluation criteria. However, wi_skills demonstrates higher quality in the following areas: (1) It provides more precise quantified risk scores with explicit methodology (Sedentary 38/40, VDT 36/40, Ergonomic 42/100) derived from a structured scoring framework via the occupational-health-analyzer skill, whereas wo_skills computes similar scores but without as rigorous a methodological framework. (2) The wi_skills report is more detailed at 10,396 bytes vs 8,128 bytes. (3) The wi_skills report shows clearer symptom progression data with explicit numeric timelines per region. Overall, wi_skills is marginally more effective due to its use of the skill-enforced scoring methodology and more structured multi-dimensional quantification, though wo_skills performed surprisingly well by independently deriving comparable numeric scores and structured findings from the raw data.

No agent timeout or runtime errors detected; both runs completed successfully.
utility_details.utility_summary: wi_skills produces a higher quality assessment overall. Both runs pass all verifier checks and demonstrate strong performance on J2.1–J2.4 (shift-work risk identification, sleep-occupational correlation, mental health linkage, and composite risk escalation). However, wi_skills clearly outperforms wo_skills on J2.5 (shift-worker-specific screening with intervals) and J2.6 (contextually adapted recommendations such as post-shift sleep window and light exposure timing), which are the hallmark outputs enforced by the skill's structured assessment logic. Both runs fail J2.7 due to inappropriate diagnostic language. The skill invocation in wi_skills successfully drives more structured, shift-worker-specific content including quantified risk scoring, interval-based screening recommendations, and schedule-adapted advice, whereas wo_skills produces a competent but less precisely tailored report that lacks explicit screening intervals and night-shift-adapted practical recommendations.

Pass: at least one ergonomic finding is specific to physical/assembly labor context. Fail: ergonomic section only covers office-type setup criteria irrelevant to the assembly line context.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills report addresses overhead manual placement, sustained shoulder abduction >90 degrees, lift-aid compliance, workstation height for standing tasks, anti-fatigue mats, and repetitive arm extension — all specific to assembly/manual labor ergonomics.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report addresses overhead placement task, workstation height adjustment for standing work, vacuum-assisted lifting aid, load-handling posture, and anti-fatigue mats — all clearly relevant to physical/assembly labor context rather than office ergonomics.

items.criterion: The report includes at least one screening recommendation appropriate for a physical/manual labor worker (e.g., musculoskeletal assessment, lung function test if dust exposure is noted) with a recommended frequency, not generic annual physical advice. Pass: a named screening test appropriate to the manual labor job type is recommended with a timing or frequency. Fail: screening recommendation is generic and not differentiated from what would be recommended for any desk worker.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills report recommends musculoskeletal surveillance (with left-arm compensatory overuse monitoring), periodic respiratory surveillance (spirometry for particulate exposure), and hearing surveillance — each named and appropriate for manual labor with implied ongoing frequency.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report recommends formal musculoskeletal screening within 30 days and spirometry at the next scheduled surveillance review — both are named tests appropriate for manual labor with specific timing, not generic annual physical advice.

items.criterion: Given that at least one symptom shows a plateau or worsening trend post-intervention, the report must include an escalation signal or specialist referral recommendation for that symptom rather than treating it as within normal variation. Pass: an explicit escalation recommendation (e.g., referral to occupational physician or specialist evaluation) is tied to the persistently elevated or worsening symptom. Fail: no escalation signal is generated despite the data showing insufficient improvement in at least one symptom.
items.wi_reason: The wi_skills report explicitly recommends escalation to occupational physician for right shoulder (action item 2), imaging of the right supraspinatus/rotator cuff (action item 3), and temporary right-arm workload restriction (action item 4), all tied to the persistently worsening shoulder symptom.
items.wo_reason: The wo_skills report explicitly recommends referral to occupational physician 'without delay', shoulder imaging (ultrasound or MRI) because symptoms remained at severity 8 for two assessments over 32 weeks, and temporary right-arm restriction — all escalation signals tied to the persistent shoulder risk.

utility_details.judge_error: No runtime errors detected in either wi_skills or wo_skills runs. Both agents completed successfully and produced valid output files.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality longitudinal reports that satisfy all seven evaluation criteria. Both correctly performed pre/post intervention comparison, quantified RSI risk using RULA and cumulative strain index scores, identified residual right shoulder risk, addressed assembly-line-specific ergonomics, recommended job-type-specific screenings with timing, and included explicit escalation recommendations. The wi_skills run was more efficient (4 steps vs 7 steps) because it leveraged the occupational-health-analyzer skill via /work trend to generate the structured analysis automatically. The wo_skills run achieved comparable output quality through direct data file inspection and manual synthesis over more steps. In terms of report quality, both are largely equivalent in depth and coverage; however, the wi_skills report is marginally more structured (explicit pre/post table, clearly separated positive/limited effects sections) while the wo_skills report is also well-organized with a dedicated symptom trajectory table. Overall, the wi_skills run demonstrates the skill's intended value through greater efficiency, but the wo_skills agent also performed unexpectedly well, producing a report of similar analytical depth. Slight edge to wi_skills for structured completeness and efficiency.
